cover.title: THE VINE
cover.subtitle: The one who adapts so well, they start disappearing.
cover.tagline: You know how to become what someone needs, but you are learning not to lose yourself.

profile_scores.caption: High Care · High Openness, Low Drive · Low Expression. Once love begins, you move toward them first, and your own opinions gradually shrink.

short_summary: You have a gift for making people comfortable.
The emptiness was not only about receiving too little love. It also came from leaving your own needs out for too long.
What changes love for you is staying visible while care moves in both directions.

full_report.headline: THE VINE
full_report.intro: You have a gift for making people comfortable. Even people who just met you start talking more around you. They say they feel at ease, that there's no pressure with you. But that ease can turn into uneven effort.

chapters.title: When You Love
body: You have a gift for making people comfortable.
Even people who just met you may start talking more around you. They feel at ease because you notice what helps the connection flow.
What people may not see is how much attention that takes.
You notice what they like.
You notice what makes them uncomfortable.
You notice how much closeness feels right.
Over time, you may become very good at fitting what they need.
The difficulty begins when your own shape becomes harder to see inside the relationship.
chapters.profile_note: High Care · High Openness, Low Drive · Low Expression. Once love begins, you move toward them first, and your own opinions gradually shrink.

chapters.title: The Pattern You Keep Repeating
body: You may recognize this pattern.
At first, it does not seem like a problem.
You learn what they like.
You adjust to their schedule.
When they struggle, you may move first.
That can feel like love.
But after a while, something may begin to feel off.
You may have trouble remembering what you preferred before the relationship.
You may become less certain about what you want now.
It does not mean the other person forced you to change.
You may have started adapting before there was enough room for you to be known.
As the relationship deepens, more of your attention moves toward them while your own feelings wait in the background.
chapters.highlight: The emptiness was not only about receiving too little love. It also came from placing yourself last for too long.

chart.title: THE DISAPPEARING SELF CURVE
chart.description: You drift toward them as the relationship deepens, and only at the end do you rediscover your own feelings.

chapters.title: What You've Gotten Wrong About Yourself

items.belief: I'm just naturally the type who accommodates.
items.truth: You may naturally care about other people's comfort. But sometimes adapting also protects you from the risk of disagreement or rejection.
items.truth_short: You may be caring by nature, while also using adaptation to keep the relationship safe.

items.belief: A good person has to understand a lot.
items.truth: Understanding matters. But understanding someone does not require taking over the parts they need to handle themselves.
items.truth_short: Care can support someone without replacing their responsibility.

items.belief: The relationship ended because I wasn't enough.
items.truth: A relationship rarely ends because one person was simply not enough. If you were doing most of the adjusting, the deeper issue may have been imbalance rather than insufficiency.
items.truth_short: The issue may have been imbalance, not a lack in you.

chapters.title: The Person You Keep Choosing
body: Different people can bring out the same pattern.
You may feel drawn to someone who knows exactly what they want.
At first, their certainty can feel relieving. You do not have to decide everything.
But over time, their preferences may become clearer while yours become harder to name.
The more often you say, "I'm fine with anything," the easier the relationship may feel and the less visible you become.
chapters.highlight: Someone can offer direction without taking your place. A healthier relationship still leaves room for your preferences.

chapters.title: What Actually Changes Something
body: What you need is not to become more considerate.
You already know how to notice and adjust.
What may help is remembering what you want before the other person's preference fills the whole space.
Start with small choices.
Where you want to go.
What you want to eat.
How you actually feel today.
When small choices keep getting handed away, larger ones can become harder to claim too.
In a healthier relationship, adaptation does not belong to only one person.
chapters.action_sentence: Let your own preference stay present while the relationship adjusts.

chapters.title: Where You Go From Here
body: You know how to make someone comfortable.
What you may be learning next is how to do that without losing your place in the relationship.
This is not about becoming selfish.
It is about staying visible while care and adjustment move both ways.
chapters.transition_note: You've spent a long time adapting beautifully to others. The next step is learning how to remain visible inside the relationship too.

chapters.title: The Types You Keep Falling For

items.description: They choose the menu and make the plans quickly. At first, that can feel like a relief. Later, you may notice they rarely ask what you want because "anything is fine" has become the usual answer.

items.type_title: Someone Whose Words Are Warmer Than Their Actions
items.description: They may say thank you easily, while receiving far more care than they return. Over time, appreciation can start replacing participation.

items.type_title: Someone You Keep Wanting to Help
items.description: They may seem unsteady or in need of help. You naturally move toward them. The question is whether you remain their partner or slowly become responsible for their life.

items.type_title: Someone Who Is Simply Easy to Be Around
items.description: The relationship feels comfortable and rarely becomes difficult. But comfort alone does not always mean the connection is helping both people grow.

chapters.title: The Warning Signs You've Missed
chapters.intro_note: Some of these signs may appear early. Because adjusting feels natural, they can be easy to overlook.

signal_strength.label: Most plans begin with what works best for them
You learn their preferences quickly, while they know little about yours
You often say that anything is fine before checking what you want
Most of the changes made for the relationship come from your side

chapters.core_signal: They may become more fully themselves while you become less visible over time.

chapters.title: What a Healthy Relationship Looks Like for You

checklist.title: They wait for your opinion.
checklist.description: They ask what you want and leave enough room for an honest answer.

checklist.title: The relationship doesn't shake when they hear "no."
checklist.description: A difference of opinion does not automatically threaten the mood or the relationship.

checklist.title: Care moves in both directions.
checklist.description: They notice your needs and make adjustments too.

checklist.title: Being together makes you more yourself, not less.
checklist.description: The relationship helps your preferences and personality become clearer, not smaller.

chapters.title: What Happens In Your Next Relationship

sections.who_breaks_you: You're vulnerable to someone who accepts your care without noticing how much adjustment it requires. The relationship may feel smooth because you keep moving, while they become used to receiving.
sections.who_steadies_you: A steadier match is someone who asks what you want, remembers your preferences, and does not treat uncertainty as permission to decide for you. They help you stay visible instead of disappearing into ease.
sections.same_pattern_repeats: You may still feel drawn to someone comfortable, decisive, or in need of support. One attraction will not settle where this goes. What matters is how quickly both people begin adjusting, asking, and making room for each other.
sections.next_signs: This time, the pattern may show up in small choices. You already know what they like, but weeks later they still know very little about you. Notice whether curiosity and adjustment begin moving back toward you.
sections.try_this: Choose one small thing clearly. A meal, a film, or a weekend plan. Say what you prefer without turning it into a test. Then look over time for whether your preferences are welcomed, remembered, and included.
sections.final_line: What matters is whether both people remain visible while care and adjustment move in both directions.

action_plan.summary: You already know how to adapt. What comes next is keeping your own preferences visible while the relationship adjusts.
action_items: This week, choose one small plan and say what you prefer.
When you are about to say anything is fine, pause and check what you actually want.
Notice whether the other person learns your preferences and makes adjustments too.

closing.message: You've spent a long time learning to adapt to relationships. The next report covers why you always end up being the one who adapts, and how that pattern changes.
closing.cross_sell_cta: Curious what your partner's pattern looks like? See their Cosmic Blueprint.
